text: This is a video presentation of a laparoscopic diverticulectomy of perforated duodenal diverticulum from the Mount Sinai Hospital in New York. The authors have no disclosures.

The duodenum is the most common place for diverticula after the colon. The prevalence is as high as twenty-two percent in autopsy series. Perforations of the duodenal diverticula are rare and present with vague and non-specific symptoms. Cases with large perforation and or systemic compromise need emergency surgery.

Our patient is a sixty years old female with history of hypothyroidism and an endoscopic center diverticulaectomy in two thousand thirteen. She presented with vague diffuse abdominal pain associated with nausea and emesis. On physical exam she got normal vital signs and a normal laboratory profile including a normal white blood count. This picture shows an esophagram with the center diverticulum managed with endoscopic diverticulaectomy in two thousand thirteen.

In the emergency department, the patient had a CT scan demonstrating free air in the retrobatonium area surrounding the area of the second and third portion of the duodenum. The patient was taken emergently to the operating room and placed in supine position on the operating table.

The surgeon accesses the abdominal cavity using a Hasson port, along with two additional 5mm ports in the right upper quadrant and a 12mm port in the left upper quadrant. An ethensor retractor is employed to retract the liver, providing a clear view of the surgical field.

During the exploration of the abdominal cavity, significant inflammation and edema are observed around the second and third portions of the duodenum. The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Cocker maneuver using a high-energy device to carefully dissect and mobilize the duodenum.

The surgeon traces the bile duct and identifies a mass of tissue adjacent to the duodenum, extending from the bile duct toward the third portion of the duodenum. During dissection, a duodenal diverticulum is observed, plastered to the second and third portions of the duodenum, with its apex bent toward the bile duct, causing the side walls to adhere to each other.
The surgeon carefully dissects the diverticulum from the duodenum using blunt maneuvers, revealing the perforation near the base of the diverticulum.

The surgeon dissects the most cephalic aspect of the diverticulum near the gallbladder, revealing the perforation with significant contents. The base of the diverticulum is being dissected using a high energy device, focusing on the most caudal aspect.

The surgeon mobilizes the diverticulum and duodenum, ensuring continuous visualization of the pancreas head. The diverticulum is seen originating from the lateral aspect of the duodenum, away from the pancreas head. Dissection continues on the lateral aspect to identify the diverticulum's base. The most cephalic aspect is dissected until the neck is identified, followed by the use of a 60mm mechanical stapler to perform the diverticulectomy.
The specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity. The surgical area is inspected, and a leak test is performed using saline and air, showing no evidence of leakage from the staple line.
The surgeon inspects the small and large bowel, revealing diverticular disease throughout. An arrow indicates the reticulum of the gestational area. A drain is placed in the subhepatic area.

On post-op day number three, the patient had an upper GI series with no evidence of leak. The patient started on clear liquid diet tolerating well and was discharged home on post-op day number six with pure antibiotics. Patient is recovered without complications. Here is the upper GI series after surgery with no evidence of leak. Thank you for your attention.
